resources.string: Emacs Tips for Two Weeks
Before diving deep, always consult the documentation, use search engines, and explore support forums. These are your first lines of help.

The Kill Ring stores text blocks that you’ve previously “killed” (cut). You can kill text in one buffer and yank (paste) it into another. Learning how the kill ring works will greatly enhance your editing workflow.
Emacs offers several terminal options like shell, eshell, shell-mode, and ansi-term. Each has unique features, so explore them to see which suits your workflow best.
Emacs is highly customizable. Use the M-x customize-themes command to change themes and make the environment feel like home. You can also tweak fonts with M-x customize-face.
Bookmark important files and positions using C-x r m to set bookmarks and C-x r b to jump to them later. It’s a huge time-saver when working on multiple projects or sections of code.
Emacs users often share their configurations online. Exploring others’ .emacs or init.el files can give you new ideas for customization and workflow improvements.

Learn to navigate the Emacs help system efficiently. Use the C-h prefix for help commands, especially C-h r for the Emacs manual and C-h s for looking up symbols. The Help menu in the toolbar is also a good place to start.
Take advantage of Emacs keyboard macros to automate tasks. As you progress, learning Elisp (Emacs Lisp) will unlock even more powerful customization and automation options.
Begin with vanilla Emacs to understand its core features. Follow System Crafter’s “Emacs from Scratch” series to build a solid foundation step by step.
The Mastering Emacs book offers great value and is worth the investment if you have the budget. Start by reading the free blog articles on Mastering Emacs to see if the style fits you.
Emacs Lisp (Elisp) is the backbone of Emacs customization. Learn Elisp through the built-in info docs, starting with the “Emacs Lisp Intro” (accessible via C-h i).
Org Mode is a powerful tool for managing notes, tasks, and more. It can replace apps like Notion and offers total control over your data, all stored in plain text.
Emacs supports multiple package repositories, including the built-in ones and MELPA. Use M-x list-packages or go to Options > Manage Emacs Packages to browse and install packages.
Emacs is best experienced as a keyboard-driven tool. Get familiar with key bindings to navigate and work efficiently.
Be Resourceful: Start with the Basics
Leverage the Power of the Kill Ring
Dive Into Built-in Terminals
Customize Everything: Themes and Fonts
Speed Up Navigation with Bookmarks
Learn from the Community: Explore Configs
Master the Emacs Help System
Automate Repetitive Tasks with Keyboard Macros
Start Simple: Vanilla Emacs with System Crafters
Invest in Learning: Mastering Emacs Book
Learn Elisp: The Golden Key
Maximize Your Workflow with Org Mode
Explore Emacs Package Repositories
Embrace the Keyboard-Driven Interface
